title: IEEE Xplore
html:
IEEE Xplore

The IEEE's academic-paper database, accessed by Yahir via the OSU library proxy in spring 2025 to pull the foundational biometrics literature for what was clearly a security/privacy seminar that semester. IEEE Xplore is the source institution behind the entire spring 2025 coursework cluster in the corpus — three screenshot-based summaries, all derived from a single canonical paper: the Jain/Pankanti/Prabhakar IEEE biometrics survey, pulled March 29 2025 and screenshotted by Yahir on April 27.

The three screenshots Xplore delivered are: 2025 04 27 screenshot 2025 04 27 at 7 40 21 pm e4e5aad8 (the modality zoo — iris, keystroke, odor, palmprint, retina, signature, voice — with each one's accuracy/spoofability/friction profile), 2025 04 27 screenshot 2025 04 27 at 7 40 38 pm 56e50e24 (the math page where FMR scales linearly with database size, plus the canonical Gartner stat "20-50% of help-desk calls are password resets, average $38/reset"), and 2025 04 27 screenshot 2025 04 27 at 7 41 04 pm 5f2b64af (the privacy/ethics page with the closing line "It is too early to predict where and how biometric technology would evolve… but it is certain that biometric-based recognition will have a profound influence on the way we conduct our daily business" — written 22 years ago, aged extremely well).

What matters about IEEE Xplore in the graph is that it's the first academic-source citation chain in the wiki — the first time Yahir's coursework reading is being kept and tagged in a way that makes the citations themselves load-bearing. The Jain survey is the textbook of biometric recognition; if Rankett or any future Yahir product touches identity verification, KYC, deepfake defense, or AI-vs-human auth, this is the foundational paper to cite. The reference list pulled out in the third screenshot — Daugman on iris (1999), Matsumoto on gummy fingers (2002), Hong & Jain on face+fingerprint fusion (1998), Kuncheva on classifier independence (2001) — is the canonical reading list around the topic.

The Felt et al. mobile-malware paper screenshotted six weeks later (2025 06 01 screenshot 2025 06 01 at 9 15 20 pm 44f00f66) is from a different source (UC Berkeley's cs.berkeley.edu/~daw/malware.html) but slots into the same security-shaped semester. IEEE Xplore is the academic backbone of the broader 2025 biometric recognition / mobile malware / surveillance state coursework cluster.
